evidence:
Deepgram's speak protocol has NO per-message context id (file header lines 14-16, design comment lines 56-60), so the plugin tracks a single mutable currentContextId. speak() sets it on every call: `this.currentContextId = contextId;` (index.ts:129). handleAudio reads it: `const contextId = this.currentContextId; if (!contextId || this.cancelledContexts.has(contextId)) return;` (index.ts:228-229). The Flushed handler also reads it instead of the ack: `case "Flushed": { const contextId = this.currentContextId; ... this.currentContextId = ""; }` (index.ts:202-211) — and ignores the `sequence_id` that the real Flushed ack carries (visible in test fixtures index.test.ts:71,239,276). So the mechanism kimi describes is real: audio/end attribution rides on a field a later speak() can overwrite.

BUT kimi's premise that 'a second tts.text before the first Flushed swings currentContextId' overstates the live window. The pipeline bus drain loop awaits each Main packet's handler before dequeuing the next (pipeline-bus.ts:205-208 `await this.dispatch`; dispatch awaits the consumer handler at :339), and the deepgram tts.text handler is registered WITHOUT `{concurrent:true}` (index.ts:106). So two speak() calls cannot interleave, and on the CLEAN back-to-back path turn B cannot even start until turn A's Flushed -> tts.end -> playback completes and the user starts a new turn — by then currentContextId was already reset to "" at index.ts:210. No overlap there.

The genuinely reachable window is BARGE-IN residual: interrupt.tts is pushed on Route.Critical (voice-agent-session.ts:956) and Critical drains before Main (pipeline-bus.ts:274), so cancelActiveContexts() runs first and synchronously sets currentContextId="" + adds turn A to cancelledContexts + sends Clear (index.ts:162-172). Then turn B's tts.text -> speak(B) sets currentContextId=B (:129). Deepgram keeps streaming a few trailing A frames until the Cleared ack arrives; in that Clear->Cleared window handleAudio reads currentContextId=B and checks cancelledContexts.has(B)=false (:229), so turn A's residual PCM is emitted tagged contextId=B with provider.cancelled=false (:248). That is a real, narrow misattribution. Contrast Cartesia, which tags every outbound frame with context_id and reads msg["context_id"] back (voice-tts-cartesia/src/index.ts:157,222) — Deepgram cannot because the protocol lacks it.
real_fix: Two layers. (1) Close the barge-in residual window: in handleAudio, suppress frames until the Cleared ack after a Clear — set a `clearedPending` flag in cancelActiveContexts() and drop audio while it is set, resetting it in the `Cleared` case (index.ts:213-215). This stops turn A's trailing PCM from being tagged with turn B. (2) Make Flushed/audio attribution robust without a per-message id by binding currentContextId transitions to send-order: only advance currentContextId to a new turn after the prior turn's Flush has been acked (or cancelled), e.g. queue contextId on Speak and pop on Flushed using the sequence_id the ack already carries (index.test.ts:71 shows `sequence_id` is present) — track a Map<sequenceId, contextId> incremented on each Flush and resolved in the Flushed handler, instead of reading the mutable currentContextId at index.ts:203. This is exactly kimi's 'track in-flight speak sequence' suggestion, grounded on the real sequence_id field. Given the only reachable overlap today is the barge-in residual, fix (1) is the high-value change; fix (2) hardens against any future turn pipelining.
files: packages/voice-tts-deepgram/src/index.ts:129 (speak sets currentContextId), :202-211 (Flushed reads currentContextId, ignores sequence_id), :227-229 (handleAudio reads currentContextId; no Clear->Cleared suppression), :213-215 (Cleared handler — add clearedPending reset here), :162-172 (cancelActiveContexts — set clearedPending here). Supporting: packages/voice/src/pipeline-bus.ts:205-208,339 (serialized drain), packages/voice/src/voice-agent-session.ts:956 (interrupt on Critical).
